summary: Gruber dismantles a New York Times piece by Tripp Mickle that entertains the possibility of manufacturing iPhones in America, arguing the article fails to simply state the obvious: it's pure fantasy. He methodically attacks the article's sourced claims — that US-assembled iPhones would cost $2,000, that Chinese women's small fingers are why Apple manufactures in China, and that the iPhone might not exist in 10 years — as unsupported nonsense laundered through analyst quotes. Gruber argues that even with extreme tariffs, it would always be cheaper for Apple to continue assembling overseas and pass costs to consumers. He notes the absurdity of the entire premise given that these are low-paying factory jobs Americans don't want, and that having these jobs overseas is a sign of American advancement, not decline. He closes by spitballing that Apple could cynically set up a tiny token assembly operation to flatter Trump into dropping tariffs.
key_points: The NYT article treats the question of US iPhone manufacturing as an open question when every fact in the piece confirms it's impossible and nonsensical
Even at 25% tariffs, passing costs to consumers ($1,000 iPhone) would always be cheaper than US assembly ($2,000), making domestic production economically irrational at any tariff level

The labor issue isn't population size but that the US lacks workers with the vocational skills willing to do tedious factory work at factory wages — having these jobs overseas signals America is ahead, not behind
The article's claim about Chinese women's small fingers being a manufacturing advantage is presented as expert opinion but is laughably ignorant
The prediction that there won't be an iPhone in 2037 is absurd given the Mac is 41 years old and still in production
money_quotes: The answer should have simply been 'Yes, it's sheer fantasy', perhaps with explanations why.
These are difficult, low-paying, long-houred jobs that Americans don't want. That these jobs are all in China and India is proof that America is far ahead, not that we've fallen behind.
If Apple passed along the entire 25% tariff on an $800 iPhone to consumers, that would raise the retail price to $1,000.
No matter how crazily high tariffs go, it only makes sense for Apple to continue assembling iPhones in China and India for now, and passing some or all of the costs along to consumers.
The Mac is 41 years old and, last I checked, Apple is still making them.
'Young Chinese women have small fingers' is in fact the dumbest argument in this entire incredibly stupid article.

Trump is so dumb, so prone to succumbing to flattery and the mere illusion that his word is others' command, and so motivated to declare victory regardless of what's actually going on, that I bet it would work.
key_insight: The question of whether iPhones could be manufactured in the US isn't worth debating — the economics make it irrational at any tariff level, and serious journalism should say so plainly rather than laundering fantasy through analyst quotes.
